diagrams.title: Peterson's Solution Two-Process Model
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing two processes (P0 and P1) accessing shared variables in the critical section problem
elements: Process P0 box (left side)
Process P1 box (right side)
Shared Memory section (center-bottom) containing: flag[2] array and turn variable
Arrow from P0 to Shared Memory labeled 'read/write flag[0] and turn'
Arrow from P1 to Shared Memory labeled 'read/write flag[1] and turn'
Entry Section (top region) for both processes
Critical Section (middle region) for both processes
Exit Section (bottom region) for both processes
Remainder Section (right side of each process box)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental two-process model where both processes share the flag array and turn variable in mutual exclusion, learning how shared variables coordinate access to critical section

diagrams.title: State Transition Diagram for Peterson's Algorithm
diagrams.description: Finite state machine showing process states while executing Peterson's solution
elements: Non-Critical Section state (outer rectangle, top)
Trying/Entry Section state (rectangle with double border, middle-left)
Critical Section state (highlighted rectangle with thick border, middle-right)
Remainder Section state (outer rectangle, bottom)
Arrow labeled 'entry protocol (flag and turn)' from Non-Critical to Trying
Arrow labeled 'enter CS' from Trying to Critical
Arrow labeled 'exit protocol (flag)' from Critical to Remainder
Arrow labeled 'repeat' from Remainder back to Non-Critical
Dotted box around Trying state labeled 'BusY-WAIT LOOP'
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how a process transitions through states, understanding the busy-wait mechanism in the entry section and the flow of execution through critical and remainder sections

diagrams.title: Peterson's Algorithm Entry Section Flow
diagrams.description: Detailed flowchart of the do-while loop showing the busy-wait mechanism
elements: Diamond decision shape labeled 'flag[other] == true AND turn == other?'
Two paths from diamond: 'YES' leads back to diamond (loop), 'NO' leads to Critical Section
Rectangle labeled 'Set flag[self] = true' before the loop
Rectangle labeled 'turn = other' before the loop
Rounded rectangle labeled 'CRITICAL SECTION' after the loop
Rectangle labeled 'flag[self] = false' in exit section
Arrow from Critical Section to Exit section
Curved arrow from YES path back to diamond (creating loop)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the exact busy-wait logic: a process waits in the entry section until the other process is not in critical section or it is its turn, understanding how mutual exclusion is achieved through the do-while construct

diagrams.title: Peterson's Solution Timing Diagram
diagrams.description: Timeline showing interleaved execution of two processes with mutual exclusion
elements: Horizontal time axis labeled t1 to t8
Two horizontal lanes: P0 (top) and P1 (bottom)
P0 lane: Entry section (shaded light gray), Critical Section (shaded dark gray with 'CS' label), Remainder (white)
P1 lane: Entry section (light gray), Critical Section (dark gray with 'CS' label), Remainder (white)
Vertical dotted lines showing time boundaries
Annotation on P0's CS: 'flag[0]=true, turn=1, flag[1]=false'
Annotation on P1's CS: 'flag[1]=true, turn=0, flag[0]=false'
Non-overlapping dark gray regions proving mutual exclusion
diagrams.purpose: Students verify visually that critical sections never overlap, demonstrating that Peterson's solution guarantees mutual exclusion. The timing diagram also shows bounded waiting as processes take turns.

charts.title: Peterson's Algorithm Execution Flowchart
charts.content: Flowchart showing the complete algorithm: (1) Process enters, sets flag[self]=true, turn=other; (2) Enter do-while loop checking flag[other]==true AND turn==other; (3) If condition true, busy-wait (loop); (4) If false, exit loop and enter Critical Section; (5) Execute CS code; (6) Set flag[self]=false in exit section; (7) Continue to remainder section; (8) Repeat. Decision diamond for loop condition, rectangles for actions, parallelogram for CS execution.

charts.title: Peterson's Solution vs Other Synchronization Mechanisms
charts.content: Comparison table with columns: Criteria | Peterson's Solution | Hardware-based (TestAndSet/Swap) | OS-based (Mutex/Semaphores). Rows: Implementation | Software-only (no special instructions) | Hardware instruction | OS kernel support. Complexity | Simple 2-variable solution | Complex atomic operations. Waiting | Busy-wait (spinlock) | Non-blocking | Block-wakeup. Performance | Wastes CPU cycles | Wastes CPU cycles | Efficient CPU usage. Starvation | Possible in strict alternation | No | Depends on implementation. Applicability | Only 2 processes | Any number | Any number.

charts.title: Properties of Peterson's Solution
charts.content: Table showing three essential properties of Peterson's solution for critical section problem: Mutual Exclusion (ensures only one process in CS at any time - achieved through flag and turn), Progress (process not in CS or waiting cannot block others - entry section has no busy loop blocking others), Bounded Waiting (limit on number of times other processes can enter CS before waiting process - achieved through turn variable). Each row includes how the property is achieved using flag[] and turn variables.

charts.title: Busy-Wait Mechanism in Entry Section
charts.content: Detailed flow of the do-while construct: Start → Set flag[i]=true (claim intent) → Set turn=j (give way to other) → While (flag[j]==true AND turn==j) {do nothing} → Enter Critical Section. The loop condition creates busy-waiting where process continuously checks if other process wants to enter and has priority. Shows that even after setting flag[i]=true, if other process set flag[j]=true first and turn=j, process i will wait. This demonstrates the 'mutual exclusion' and 'progress' properties.

mindMaps.centralConcept: Peterson's Solution for Critical Section Problem
branches: Algorithm Components (Shared Variables): flag[2] - boolean array indicating process intent, turn - indicates whose turn it is
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting Peterson's solution to critical section problem, showing its components, properties, advantages, limitations, and relationship to other synchronization concepts. Branch 1: Components (flag and turn variables with their purpose). Branch 2: Properties (Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting). Branch 3: Working (Busy-wait do-while loop, Entry/Exit protocols). Branch 4: Advantages (Software-only, no hardware primitives). Branch 5: Limitations (Only 2 processes, busy-wait CPU waste, works only for uniprocessor). Branch 6: Related Concepts (TestAndSet, Swap, Semaphores, Monitor).

branches: Critical Section Problem: Shared resource, Entry section, CS, Exit section, Remainder section
Peterson's Solution: Two-process mutual exclusion algorithm using flag[] and turn
Synchronization Tools: Hardware-based (TestAndSet, Swap), Software-based (Peterson), OS-based (Semaphores, Monitors)
Properties Required: Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting
Real-world Applications: Database concurrency control, Multi-threaded programs, Operating system kernel synchronization
mindMaps.purpose: Shows how Peterson's solution fits into the broader domain of process synchronization, connecting it to the critical section problem it solves, other synchronization mechanisms, required properties, and practical applications in operating systems and concurrent programming.
